concept.conceptName: Aspartate aminotransferase|Catalytic Concentration|Moment in time|Blood, Serum or Plasma

en.name: Aspartate aminotransferase (AST) in blood
en.longDescription: ## Definition & Clinical Context Aspartate aminotransferase (AST, also called SGOT — serum glutamic oxaloacetic transaminase) is an enzyme released by injured muscle cells and liver cells, predominantly found in the liver and the heart [1]. AST is part of every liver panel, typically measured together with ALT to assess hepatocellular injury; an AST/ALT ratio above 2 is a classical pattern of alcoholic liver disease, but AST is less liver-specific than ALT (also rises in muscle damage, myocardial infarction, haemolysis) [1]. The source data will typically be a single numeric result per patient-timestamp, produced by a biochemistry analyser on serum or plasma. Results are reported as catalytic activity concentration; NPU recognises both **U/L** (the conventional unit, used by virtually all clinical labs) and **µkat/L** (the SI unit) [2]. The IFCC reference measurement procedure for AST at 37 °C, listed by JCTLM, gives the conversion factor `1 U/L = 0.01667 µkat/L` [3]. **Note on assay method (with vs without P-5'-P).** Pyridoxal-5'-phosphate (P-5'-P) is the cofactor of AST. The IFCC primary reference procedure adds P-5'-P to the reagent to activate the enzyme that is otherwise present as inactive apo-AST, giving the total AST activity [4]. Assays run without added P-5'-P measure only the holo-fraction already saturated in the sample, which can underestimate total activity (especially in patients deficient in vitamin B6). Both methods produce results expressed in U/L; values are not strictly interchangeable across the two methods, but LOINC treats them as the same measurand under different method specifications. ## Included Concepts The set contains three LOINC "Lab Test" concepts, all measuring AST catalytic activity in serum or plasma, differing only by whether P-5'-P is added. ### Generic (method not specified) The default when the source does not state which method is used. - *3013721 / 1920-8 — Aspartate aminotransferase [Enzymatic activity/volume] in Serum or Plasma (LOINC)*: most widely used LOINC code, returned by routine biochemistry analysers when the method is not detailed. ### Method-specific (with or without P-5'-P) Use these only when the source explicitly identifies the assay method. - *3037081 / 30239-8 — Aspartate aminotransferase [Enzymatic activity/volume] in Serum or Plasma by With P-5'-P (LOINC)*: IFCC-recommended method. - *36305398 / 88112-8 — Aspartate aminotransferase [Enzymatic activity/volume] in Serum or Plasma by No addition of P-5'-P (LOINC)*: economy method, still common in practice. **Note on units.** Recommended unit follows LOINC: **`[U]/L`** (concept 8645). The notations `U/L` and `IU/L` are used interchangeably in clinical practice. ## Excluded Concepts No concepts are explicitly excluded. The set is defined by anchoring on the LOINC group for AST catalytic activity in blood/serum/plasma and pulling in all its descendants. This naturally excludes: - **Aggregations** like `Aspartate aminotransferase ... (Maximum value during study)` (`44786-2`) — out of scope. - **Non-blood specimens** such as red blood cells (`16412-9`). - **Macromolecular AST** (`48136-6`, `100739-2`, `54500-4`) — qualitative or research test for macro-AST complexes. - **Presence-only test** (`27344-1`) — qualitative, not a quantitative activity measurement. - **Ratios** such as AST/ALT (`1916-6`), ALT/AST (`16325-3`), GGT/AST (`2325-9`) — out of scope. Notably, no LOINC concepts exist for `AST in Blood` or `AST in Serum, Plasma or Blood` (unlike ALT and ALP), because AST is essentially never reported on whole blood in routine practice. ## Mapping Notes - **Default concept** when the source reports "AST", "SGOT", "ASAT" or similar without further specification (most routine biochemistry analysers): `3013721 / 1920-8 — Aspartate aminotransferase [Enzymatic activity/volume] in Serum or Plasma`. By far the most common. - **Source explicitly states P-5'-P method**: map to `3037081 / 30239-8` (with P-5'-P) or `36305398 / 88112-8` (without P-5'-P) accordingly. Otherwise, default to `1920-8` — do **not** infer the method from the analyser brand or the country. - **Do not confuse with**: ALT (alanine aminotransferase, a related but distinct enzyme), AST/ALT and ALT/AST ratios, macromolecular AST, or AST in non-blood specimens (e.g. red blood cells) — all belong to other concept sets. - **Method is not part of the mapping target beyond P-5'-P**: when the source records additional method details (kinetic, malate dehydrogenase coupling, etc.), preserve them in the measurement source value — they do not change the target concept. - **Unit naming**: source systems may report `U/L` or `IU/L`. Both correspond to the same physical quantity. Map them all to the recommended unit `[U]/L` (concept 8645). Values reported in `µkat/L` should be converted using `1 U/L = 0.01667 µkat/L` (i.e. multiply µkat/L by ~60 to get U/L) [3]. ## References [1] Regenstrief Institute. LOINC code 1920-8 — Aspartate aminotransferase [Enzymatic activity/volume] in Serum or Plasma (Part description for "Aspartate aminotransferase"). Available: https://loinc.org/1920-8/ [2] Nomenclature for Properties and Units (NPU). IFCC/IUPAC. NPU codes (current release). Available: https://npu-terminology.org/npu-database/ [3] Joint Committee for Traceability in Laboratory Medicine (JCTLM). Database of higher-order reference measurement procedures, reference materials and reference measurement services. BIPM/IFCC/ILAC. Available: https://www.jctlmdb.org/#/app/home [4] Schumann G, Bonora R, Ceriotti F, Férard G, Ferrero CA, Franck PFH, Gella FJ, Hoelzel W, Jørgensen PJ, Kanno T, Kessner A, Klauke R, Kristiansen N, Lessinger JM, Linsinger TPJ, Misaki H, Panteghini M, Pauwels J, Schiele F, Schimmel HG, Weidemann G, Siekmann L. IFCC primary reference procedures for the measurement of catalytic activity concentrations of enzymes at 37 °C. Part 5. Reference procedure for the measurement of catalytic concentration of aspartate aminotransferase. Clin Chem Lab Med. 2002;40(7):725-733. Available: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/12241022/
